channel.title: Workshop03
channel.generator: Webpage HTML Export plugin for Obsidian
channel.dc:creator: jmgcfdea

item.title: Algorithms for Bioinformatics
item.description:
Path: Notes/Bioinformatics/Introdução à Bioinformárica/Algorithms for Bioinformatics.md
Created: 2024-09-13 15:10
Updated: 2024-09-13 15:10
Source: Algorithms for Bioinformatics
Subject: Algorithms for Bioinformatics
Tags:
Ontology parent::Program Biological Sequences molecular mass Nucleic acid melting temperature CpG island and isochore detection k-mer counting Regular expression Hidden Markov model Position-specific scoring matrix Genetic engineering Quality control report Probes and primers PCR primer design


Definition An algorithm is a specific set of instructions for carrying out a procedure or solving a problem, usually with the requirement that the procedure terminate at some point. Specific algorithms sometimes also go by the name method, procedure, or technique. The word "algorithm" is a distortion of al-Khwārizmī, a Persian mathematician who wrote an influential treatise about algebraic methods. Source: Wolfram MathWorld



The occurrence of a feature is counted (or measured) along the sequence from end to end.
Case Studies
G + C Content
Skeleton structure
Practical application
Melting Temperature (Tm) calculation
Example formula
Paradigm inversion
Once upon a time... Tm was measured for %G+C calculation
Example Implementation
EMBOSS::GeeCee
Through mobyle portal
Molecular Mass
Skeleton structure
Example Implementation
ExPASY::ProtParam
Protparam output


When dealing with localized features in order to detect local occurrence.
When the sought feature is found the (Account) Balance is incremented (Credit)
If it is not found he (Account) Balance is decremented (Debit)
The site boundary begins when Balance starts growing from a local minimum.
The site ends when the Balance stops growing.
Balance progression
Case Study
GpC Island Detection
CpG Island
What is... Involved in resistance to methylation “associated with the 5' ends of most”(mammalian)” house-keeping genes and many regulated genes (Bird, 1986, Larsen et al 1992)”
General characteristics
> 200 bp
%G+C = 50%
Example Implementation
EMBOSS::cpgreport


The sequence is considered n-odd residues at a time, the so called Window.
At each iteration
This Window slides one site towards the end of the sequence
Sliding along the sequence
The properties of the residues inside the Window are considered for calculation purposes
This calculation is typically based on a given Metric (scoring scheme).
Each calculated value encompasses both the site where the Window is centered into, and its neighbourhood.
Case Studies
Vocabulary Analysis
Hashing along the sequence
Get and count all the possible Words of a given length.
Heptamers
Vocabulary can be crossed with a priori probability.
Local assessment for a given property
Property profile
Distribution along the sequence
.
Example Implementation
ExPASy::ProtScale


Graphical comparison of sequences
Dot Plot
Internal comparison for a sequence
.
Looking further into the Dot-Plot technique (MANDATORY.)


The Sequence is considered as a putative set of Patterns
Usually defined as a String
Definition .
Mostly as a perfect match between String and substring.
To a lesser degree, matches may be considered if partial or ambiguous
Wildcards
Regular expressions
[PSSM] (http://edamontology.org/data_2854)
HMM
Case Study
Conceptual Restriction
Search for string patterns identical to recognition sites for type II Endonucleases

Pattern occurrence
Implies existence of cutting site(s)
Enzyme annotation
Rebase (NEB)
.
Sample entry
.
Intended Use
Forecast of useful endonucleases for building genetic engineering constructs.
Quality Control of ordered sequencing data.
Example Implementation
NebCutter (NEB)



Primer
Definition Source: Wikipedia
.
Primer Design
Optimizing relevant parameters for specificity
Length
Cost
Composition
Specificity
Tm
Self-complementarity
Location
The closest possible to the target sequence
Multidimensional optimization
Example Implementation
Primer3
.
Sample output
.
item.dc:creator: jmgcfdea

item.title: Biological Sequences
item.description:
Path: Notes/Bioinformatics/Introdução à Bioinformárica/Biological Sequences.md
Created: 2024-09-03 15:51
Updated: 2024-09-03 15:51
Source: Biological Sequences
Subject: Biological Sequences
Tags:
Ontology parent::Program Sequence Nucleic acids Proteins RNA DNA Format Sequence annotation Sequencing Scaffolding

EDAM::data_2044

Definition “Polymers are very large molecules made of repeating patterns of small molecules, called monomers. Many polymers are made by repeating the same small molecule over and over again. Others are made from two monomers linked in a pattern.”
Source    


Sugars, e.g cellulose
Amino-acids, e.g. proteins
Nucleotides, e.g. nucleic acids


Structural, e.g. proteins, cellulose
Enzymatic Activity, e.g. proteins
Storage, e.g. glycogen
Information Transmission, e.g. RNA, proteins
Information Storage, e.g. nucleic acids


Linear
Sequence of residues
Branched
Network



The polymer is an existing substance with properties, structure and possible roles in the cell or otherwise
The sequence is a limited representation of the existing molecule.
Only the sequential order of the residues is portrayed.
Implications of that sequential order are up to the reader of the sequence.
The way residues are represented depends on the intended use or volition of the sequence coder.



The sequence as an information container.
The sequential order of residues is the information contained
Other features conveying information
Modifications to the residues
Molecule folding
Association with other concomitant molecules
How to measure the information content in a naive approach?
Deviation to a random result
Looking at the sequence as a sequence of "choices" between available states
Assessing the degree of "organization" conveyed by the sequence
Information content varies throughout gene expression




Notation
Symbols for residue representation along the sequence being recorded.
Definite residues
Ambiguous situations
Ill defined residue occurrence
Polymorphic site (e.g. SNP)
IUPAC
Nucleotide Notation

Amino-Acid Notation




Format
Definition A defined way or layout of representing and structuring data in a computer file, blob, string, message, or elsewhere.
Source: EDAM::format_1915
.
Sequence recording formats
Separate sequences
RAW
Only sequence as a single line
FASTA / Pearson
First line
Sequence annotation
Second line
Sequence

Specialized formats
Phylip

NBRF / PIR

GDE

Aligned sequences
Sequence alignment in a nutshell
Mathematical technique for assessing homologous sites across different, but related sequences

Main formats
Clustal

FASTA / Pearson

Exquisite formats
GCG / MSF

Codata

The definitive format?
NEXUS
A modular format that can encompass a complete phylogenetic analysis
Modules go from the annotation for the individual sequences, through their multiple alignment, distance matrix up to complete sets of trees resulting from bootstrap analysis, and the resulting consensus tree.

...

Blocks for specific elements
Taxa (name + annotation)

Sequences

Alignment

Distance matrix

Phylogenetic trees



Sequences are usually recorded manually as a plain text through a regular text editor

Software also records sequences as output
As no human is involved, formats can be more complex
Fluorograms resulting from sequencing DNA can also be regarded as a form of sequence recording




Biopolymer sequencing
DNA versus Protein
Completely different analytical concepts
Residue position through template
Residue chemical properties
DNA Sequencing (~50 years record)
Methodology
Chemical - Maxam & Gilbert

Enzymatic - Sanger

Conceptual foundation for most of the subsequent developments
PCR revolution (~40 years record)
Increased agility
Robotization
Next Generation Sequencing (NGS)

Read Assembly

The process limiting step
Higher parallelization => shorter reads
Reads are the building blocks in Genome Assembly

Read
Contig
Scaffold
Chromosome
Protein Sequencing (~70years record)
Direct sequencing
Classical methods
Work intensive
Not amenable to automatisation
Mass spectroscopy based methods
Based on peptide signature recognition
"Black Box"
Not completely deterministic
Conceptual Translation Inference
Genomic sequence annotation
Automatic translation of inferred coding sequences (CDS)
Abundant source for debatable protein sequences
Most available protein sequences generated through this process
item.dc:creator: jmgcfdea

item.title: Dot-Plot
item.description:
Path: Notes/Bioinformatics/Introdução à Bioinformárica/Dot-Plot.md
Created: 2024-09-14 09:09
Updated: 2024-09-14 09:09
Source: Dot-Plot
Subject: Dot-Plot
Tags:
Ontology parent::Algorithms for Bioinformatics




n-tuple / k-mer
Definition 'k-mers are substrings of length 𝑘 {\displaystyle k} contained within a biological sequence.'


n / k - The Word Size
The higher the value, the more significant
Stochastic probability for any word of lenght k .
Both senses should be considered
For k-mer matching
Direct + Inverted
Robustness
Definition "transposed into a system, it refers to the ability of tolerating perturbations" .
Function of varying
Window size
Concurrent parameters
e.g. Tolerance
Number of accepted mismatches between two windows that does not preclude displaying a dot.


Scanning the space of the available comparisons
For k-mers
All against all
Graphical display for higher scoring results
Sliding down






Next col

...
.


Signal
Definition "The signal is the meaningful information that you’re actually trying to detect."
Source: [conceptually.org] (https://conceptually.org/concepts/signal-and-noise)
.
Diagonals
The longer, the better
Robustness as a benchmark
Noise
Definition "The noise is the random, unwanted variation or fluctuation that interferes with the signal."
Source: [conceptually.org] (https://conceptually.org/concepts/signal-and-noise)
.
Short diagonals
From dots to (really) short diagonals
Signal to Noise Ratio
Definition .
Dot-Plot-wise

.


Axes
Meant Sequences
Either the same
Search for internal repetitions
Or different
similarity distribution between them
Diagonals (signal)
Connect similar stretches shared by both sequences
Main diagonal or similar
Partial translations along one axis
Case study
Multiple internal (direct) repetitions
Interpretation
Source: MyHits(SIB)::dotlet THIS IS MANDATORY READING!



MyHits(SIB)::Dotlet JS
The most useful although no Tolerance is used

EMBOSS::dotmatcher
Uses Tolerance as a parameter
Non interactive.
item.dc:creator: jmgcfdea

item.title: Hands-on Class - AP02 - Algorithms in Action
item.description:
Path: Notes/Bioinformatics/Introdução à Bioinformárica/Hands-on Class - AP02 - Algorithms in Action.md
Created: 2024-09-17 16:09
Updated: 2024-09-17 16:09
Source: Hands-on Class - AP02 - Algorithms in Action
Subject: Hands-on Class - AP02 - Algorithms in Action
Tags:
Ontology parent::Program Algorithms for Bioinformatics




Input Sequence
Aj786248.1 Source: INSDC::GenBank
.
Suggested Tool
NebCutter (NEB)
- >
> .
>


Submit each sequence to NebCutter
Obtain a result for endonucleases that only cut twice along the sequence
Repeat the operation for enzymes with a pattern of at least 6 bp.
Look for the:
Restriction map.
Select 2 enzymes compatible with double digestion
Electroforesis simulated run.
Select a suitable
agarose concentration
Molecular weight markers



Input Sequence
Ab007638.1 Source: EBI::ENA Look for annotation at NCBI .
Suggested Tool
NCBI::ORFinder
- >
> .


Submit AB007638.1 to ORF Finder.
Choose a Genetic Code before proceeding with the analysis
Check the Genetic Codes support page before deciding.
Compare the generated ORF set with the refined annotation available at NCBI.



Input Sequence
Ab007638.1 Source: EBI::ENA
Suggested Tool
Augustus
- >
> .


Upload the same sequence.
Choose the closest model available.
Scan both strands
No alternative transcripts
Predict only complete genes
Try to identify the output format
If possible, visualize the result graphically (load annotation and sequence).
Suggestion Source: GenomeView
Compare this ab initio result with the former annotations.
item.guid: Notes/Bioinformatics/Introdução à Bioinformática/Hands-on Class - AP02 - Algorithms in Action.md
item.dc:creator: jmgcfdea

item.title: Hands-on Class - AP03 - Algorithms in Action
item.description:
Path: Notes/Bioinformatics/Introdução à Bioinformárica/Hands-on Class - AP02 - Algorithms in Action.md
Created: 2024-09-16 11:48
Updated: 2024-09-16 11:48
Source: Hands-on Class - AP03 - Algorithms in Action
Subject: Hands-on Class - AP03 - Algorithms in Action
Tags:
Ontology parent::Algorithms for Bioinformatics Program



Input Sequences
X05076.1 Source: GenBank
P05130 Source: UniProt
.


ibi24_Protocol4AP02.pdf

item.title: Hands-on Session_Pairwise Alignment
item.description:
Path: Notes/Bioinformatics/Introdução à Bioinformática/Hands-on Session_Pairwise Alignment.md
Created: 2024-09-30 18:24
Updated: 2024-09-30 18:24
Source: Hands-on Session_Pairwise Alignment
Subject: Hands-on Session_Pairwise Alignment
Tags:
Ontology parent::Program Pairwise Alignment, wrapping up



>Seq_01 MAPGQLALFSVSDKTGLVEFARNLTALGLNLVASGGTAKALRDAGLAVRDVSELTGFPEM LGGRVKTLHPAVHAGILARNIPEDNADMARLDFNLIRVVACNLYPFVKTVASPGVTVEEA VEQIDIGGVTLLRAAAKNHARVTVVCEPEDYVVVSTEMQSSESKDTSLETRRQLALKAFT HTAQYDEAISDYFRKQYSKGVSQMPLRYGMNPHQTPAQLYTLQPKLPITVLNGAPGFINL CDALNAWQLVKELKEALGIPAAASFKHVSPAGAAVGIPLSEDEAKVCMVYDLYKTLTPIS AAYARARGADRMSSFGDFVALSDVCDVPTAKIISREVSDGIIAPGYEEEALTILSKKKNG NYCVLQMDQSYKPDENEVRTLFGLHLSQKRNNGVVDKSLFSNVVTKNKDLPESALRDLIV ATIAVKYTQSNSVCYAKNGQVIGIGAGQQSRIHCTRLAGDKANYWWLRHHPQVLSMKFKT GVKRAEISNAIDQYVTGTIGEDEDLIKWKALFEEVPELLTEAEKKEWVEKLTEVSISSDA FFPFRDNVDRAKRSGVAYIAAPSGSAADKVVIEACDELGIILAHTNLRLFHH Copy
>Seq_02 MAVTVICDPNDYEGVSHEILSSPSRQTSLDTRRRLAVKAFLHTAQYDAAISDYFRKQYLQ GVAQLPLRYGMNPHQVPAQLCTSLPQLPLKVVNGAPGFINLCDALNAWQLVRELKAALGL YDILTPIATAYARARGADRMSSFGDFLTPEALEILKKKKGGNYCVLQMDPTYEPALLESR TLFGLTLEQRRNDARINKSLFDNIVSKNKALPEPAVRDLIVATIALKYTQSNSVCYAKDG QVIGIGAGQQSRIHCTRLAGDKANNWWLRQHPNIKSMVFRKGVKRAEISNVIDVYVGGVF QQSRIHCTRLAGDKANYWWLRHHPQVLSMWFKTWVMLSSDAFFPFRDNIDRAHQTGVQYI ASPGGSTNDQGVIEACDEYGITLCHTGLRLFHH Copy


Dotlet JS

EMBOSS (EMBL, Univ Waggeningen)

FASTA (EBI)

BLAST (EBI)



Store locally the supplied sequences.
Compare visually the similarity shared betwee the sequences.
Using tools from the EMBOSS package for the following tasks.
Choose for the output in all of them the format
EMBOSS Pairwise
Align both sequences using the Needleman-Wunsch algorithm.
Assess their similarity, and decide on the best BLOSUM matrix to be used
Looking for available blosum matrices
.
Repeat the process using an implementation of the Myers-Miller algorithm. Scan the documentation for that purpose.
Compare both global alignment solutions.
Now, changing the alignment paradigm, align both sequences through the Smith-Water algorithm.

.
Consider the yielded similarity level, and consider if the substitution matrix should be reconsidered
Next, locate an implementation for the Waterman-Eggert algorithm. Run an alignment, but ask for the 3 best solutions.
Check if the solutions are mutually exclusive.
Compare the best one with the solution from the 8.
From now on, the procedure is to be applied just to the first sequence.
Use that sequence as a query to search the database UNIPROT::SwissProt through both FASTA and BLAST.
Locate and open "Tool Output" panel.
Store that textual output.
Consider the best results for each query:
Look at the identity found
Recover the target sequence
Align globally both the query and the target
Compare either the FASTA or BLAST similarities to the latter.
Locate the e-value column.
item.dc:creator: jmgcfdea

item.title: Hands-on Session_Proteins, sequences & algorithms
item.description:
Path: Notes/Bioinformatics/Introdução à Bioinformárica/Hands-on Session_Proteins, sequences & algorithms.md
Created: 2024-09-23 10:54
Updated: 2024-09-23 10:54
Source: Hands-on Session_Proteins, sequences & algorithms
Subject: Hands-on Session_Proteins, sequences & algorithms
Tags:
Ontology parent::Program Protein Sequence Analysis


This hands-on session is part of a procedure that will continued at a later session.
Students are invited to keep an individual record for each one of the sequences being analyzed.
Students are strongly encouraged to rely on text-only record, both for input, and output.
This focus the student in the real nature of the raw tool output, and allows for a more flexible exploitation of results.
For alternative approaches, note-taking or spreadsheet software are recommended.


>Sequence_1 MDLKESPSEGSLQPSSIQIFANTSTLHGIRHIFVYGPLTIRRVLWAVAFVGSLGLLLVE SSERVSYYFSYQHVTKVDEVVAQSLVFPAVTLCNLNGFRFSRLTTNDLYHAGELLALLD VNLQIPDPHLADPSVLEALRQKANFKHYKPKQFSMLEFLHRVGHDLKDMMLYCKFKGQE CGHQDFTTVFTKYGKCYMFNSGEDGKPLLTTVKGGTGNGLEIMLDIQQDEYLPIWGETE ETTFEAGVKVQIHSQSEPPFIQELGFGVAPGFQTFVATQEQRLTYLPPPWGECRSSEMG LDFFPVYSITACRIDCETRYIVENCNCRMVHMPGDAPFCTPEQHKECAEPALGLLAEKD SNYCLCRTPCNLTRYNKELSMVKIPSKTSAKYLEKKFNKSEKYISENILVLDIFFEALN YETIEQKKAYEVAALLGDIGGQMGLFIGASILTILELFDYIYELIKEKLLDLLGKEEDE GSHDENVSTCDTMPNHSETISHTVNVPLQTTLGTLEEIAC Copy
>Sequence_2 MELKTEEEEVGGVQPVSIQAFASSSTLHGLAHIFSYERLSLKRALWALCFLGSLAVLLC VCTERVQYYFCYHHVTKLDEVAASQLTFPAVTLCNLNEFRFSQVSKNDLYHAGELLALL NNRYEIPDTQMADEKQLEILQDKANFRSFKPKPFNMREFYDRAGHDIRDMLLSCHFRGE ACSAEDFKVVFTRYGKCYTFNSGQDGRPRLKTMKGGTGNGLEIMLDIQQDEYLPVWGET DETSFEAGIKVQIHSQDEPPFIDQLGFGVAPGFQTFVSCQEQRLIYLPSPWGTCNAVTM DSDFFDSYSITACRIDCETRYLVENCNCRMVHMPGDAPYCTPEQYKECADPALDFLVEK DQEYCVCEMPCNLTRYGKELSMVKIPSKASAKYLAKKFNKSEQYIGENILVLDIFFEVL NYETIEQKKAYEIAGLLGDIGGQMGLFIGASILTVLELFDYAYEVIKHRLCRRGKCQKE AKRSSADKGVALSLDDVKRHNPCESLRGHPAGMTYAANILPHHPARGTFEDFTC Copy


ExPASy::ProtParam

ExPASy::PeptideCutter

Phobius (SBC)

DeepTMHMM (TUD)

PSIPRED (UCL)

COILS (PRABI)



Store locally the supplied sequences (folder, file and format)
Compare both sequences visually and record the main differences between both sequences
Determine to the best of your abilities
Molecular mass
Isoelectric point
Imagine that you are keen on design a discriminant test for both proteins.
Elect a tool to explore cutting agents that may cut both proteins
No more than twice
One agent that may yield fragments with length in line with the proportions
1 - 3 - 6
The same agent must cut(?) the other protein in a rather different way
Being a membrane bound protein or not (globular) is rather relevant
Look for signs of transmembrane regions for each protein
Use several tools
Look for concordance between their results
Map them to the sequence
Search for support evidence for any putative transmembrane region
Look int the inferred secondary structure
Add evidence for any hits on the existence of quaternary structure
Coiled-coils regions
Disordered regions with binding potential
item.guid: Notes/Bioinformatics/Introdução à Bioinformática/Hands-on Session_Proteins, sequences & algorithms.md
item.dc:creator: jmgcfdea

item.title: HMM - Hidden Markov Models
item.description:
Path: Notes/Bioinformatics/Introdução à Bioinformárica/HMM - Hidden Markov Models.md
Created: 2024-09-20 16:14
Updated: 2024-09-20 16:14
Source: HMM - Hidden Markov Models
Subject: HMM - Hidden Markov Models
Tags:
Ontology parent::Program Protein Sequence Analysis



A door to the invisible
Observable states
The input data
Confusion Matrix
Connecting Observable to Hidden states
Levels of probability
Resulting from Training
Set of "solved" inputs
Hidden states
States of interest that the system may assume
Non-observable
Categorized through levels of
Posterior Probability

.
From the realm of atmospherics - an example
Weather in Iberia is controlled by the global atmospheric circulation

.
Either High or Low pressure cells are invisible to the naked eye, even considering cloud patterns
Their distribution, and movement are the direct cause of observable weather conditions

.
A rather simple model can relate both layers of states

Using the past meteorological data record, the model can be trained to reveal the
More probable current Hidden state
Given the present Observable state
item.guid: Notes/Bioinformatics/Introdução à Bioinformática/HMM - Hidden Markov Models.md
item.dc:creator: jmgcfdea

item.title: Introdução à Bioinformática
item.description:
#undidadeCurricular #lbcm #jmgcfdea
Path: Notes/Bioinformatics/Introdução à Bioinformárica/Introdução à Bioinformática.md
Created: 2024-09-03 15:47
Updated: 2024-09-03 15:47
Source: Introdução à Bioinformática
Subject: Introdução à Bioinformática
Tags: #undidadeCurricular #lbcm #jmgcfdea
Ontology parent::Bioinformatics

EDAM::topic_0091
Definition The archival, curation, processing and analysis of complex biological data.
This includes data processing in general, including basic handling of files and databases, datatypes, workflows and annotation.
Source: EDAM

Bioinformatics in time Source: Briefings in Bioinformatics
item.dc:creator: jmgcfdea

item.title: Ontologies & Knowledge Representation
item.description:
Path: Notes/Bioinformatics/Introdução à Bioinformárica/Ontologies & Knowledge Representation.md
Created: 2024-09-05 10:18
Updated: 2024-09-05 10:18
Source: Ontologies & Knowledge Representation
Subject: Ontologies & Knowledge Representation
Tags:
Ontology parent::Program

Definition "an ontology is the description of what exist specifically within a determined field. For example, every part that exists in a specific information system. This includes the relationship and hierarchy between these parts."
Kent Lofgren
The whole concept of ontology





Fonte: EDAM


Knowledge Representation and Ontologies.pdf



What Is a Knowledge Representation.pdf
item.dc:creator: jmgcfdea

item.title: Pairwise Alignment, wrapping up
item.description:
Path: Notes/Bioinformatics/Introdução à Bioinformática/Pairwise Alignment, wrapping up .md
Created: 2024-09-30 16:34
Updated: 2024-09-30 16:34
Source: Pairwise Alignment, wrapping up
Subject: Pairwise Alignment, wrapping up
Tags:
Ontology parent::program Sequence Pairwise-Alignment Fast Algorithms, Heuristics to the Rescue



Alignment Paradigm / Type
Global
Local
Alignment Algorithm Type
Slow
Without Heuristics involvement
Fast
Heuristics
Sometimes, dynamic programming also
Common reference
.


Global Alignment
Aims
Comparison of sequences that share several conserved regions and are of comparable length
Polymorphism mapping
Sequence version comparison
Example Implementations
Needleman & Wunsch
EMBOSS::needle
Myers & Miller
EMBOSS::stretcher
FASTA::align(0)
Local Alignment
Aims
Localized similarity
Local similarity in repeats
Example Implementations
Smith-Waterman
EMBOSS::water
Waterman-Eggert
EMBOSS::matcher


Approaches
Brute force?
Try until you find.
Map similarity between the two sequences
Dot-Plot
Qualitative result
Example Implementations
Dotter (Karolinska Inst.)
The original tool
Command line
JDotter VBRC, Univ. Victoria
Dotter on a server with a JAVA GUI
Severe limitations regarding data input
Dotlet (SIB)
Dotter on a server with a JAVA applet – LEGACY
Applets were phased out from modern browsers
Dotlet JS beta (SIB)
Modern implementation for Dotlet
Not all Dotlet capabilities were ported to JS
Namely concurrent reverse window matching
"Tolerance" effect through score range representation

.
Taming the Beast
Two humps, and the Bactrian Camel
Output
.
Scoring range
.
Hence the bactrian camel
.
Separating Noise from Signal
The sweet spot
.
Reversing the Background
Swapping cursors
.
item.guid: Notes/Bioinformatics/Introdução à Bioinformática/Pairwise Alignment, wrapping up.md
item.dc:creator: jmgcfdea

item.title: Pairwise-Alignment, the Dynamic Programming Role
item.description:
Path: Notes/Bioinformatics/Introdução à Bioinformárica/Pairwise-Alignment, the Dynamic Programming Role.md
Created: 2024-09-27 04:54
Updated: 2024-09-27 04:54
Source: Pairwise-Alignment, the Dynamic Programming Role
Subject: Pairwise-Alignment, the Dynamic Programming Role
Tags:
Ontology parent::Program Sequence Alignment



Sequence alignment
Optimization of the superposition of sites
Guiding by a metric
Substitution matrix
Goal
Maximizing the score

.
Possible approaches
Monte-Carlo sampling
Example

.

.

.

.
Non-efficient
Prone to stumble on local maxima
Ignoring the global maximum score
Dynamic programming
Efficient solution to evaluate the whole solution space


Devised by Bellman (1957) to address the optimization of dynamic system modelling
The paradigm of Optimum Management (Optionality)
Based on the principle "divide et impera" (divide and rule)
An intractable problem is divided in a set of component problems
Each component problem is solved
Their solution contribute to a global solution for the initial problem

.
The oldest implementation of this class of algorithms for pairwise-alignment
Needleman-Wunsch Algorithm (1970)



Filling the scores matrix
Scores matrix using identity matrix
.
Score accumulation
Accumulation starts from a corner
.
Second column and row The maximum of the submatrix is added to the existing score.
.
Going forward
.

.

.

.

.

.

.
Diminishing scores path


Possible solutions
.


Local alignment based on the Dynamic Programming paradigm (1981)
.

Filling the scores matrix
Scores matrix using identity matrix
.
Score accumulation
Accumulation starts from a corner
.
Accumulation favors prior diagonals Only the corner of the submatrix is considered for the accumulation.
.

.
Decreasing scores path
.
Resulting solutions
.
item.guid: Notes/Bioinformatics/Introdução à Bioinformática/Pairwise-Alignment, the Dynamic Programming Role.md
item.dc:creator: jmgcfdea

item.title: Program
item.description:
Path: Notes/Bioinformatics/Introdução à Bioinformárica/Programa.md
Created: 2024-09-02 14:55
Updated: 2024-09-02 14:55
Source: Program
Subject: Program
Tags:
Ontology parent::Introdução à Bioinformática




Topic 0: Ontologies & Knowledge Representation


Lecture 1: Biological Sequences
Support Topic 1: Relational Databases
Hands-on Session 1: Protocolo_1_Registo_Armazenamento_Sequências
Lecture 2: Algorithms for Bioinformatics
Additional Topic 2: Translated Regions Detection
Hands-on Session 2: Hands-on Class - AP02 - Algorithms in Action
Lecture 3: Protein Sequence Analysis
Additional Topic 3a: Sequence Evolution
Additional Topic 3b: Sequence Alignment
Hands-on Session 3: Hands-on Session_Proteins, sequences & algorithms
Lecture 4: The Substitution Matrix
Additional Topic 4a: Pairwise-Alignment, the Dynamic Programming Role
Additional Topic 4b: Sequence Pairwise-Alignment Fast Algorithms, Heuristics to the Rescue
Additional Topic 4c: Pairwise Alignment, wrapping up
Hands-on Session 4: Hands-on Session_Pairwise Alignment
item.dc:creator: jmgcfdea

item.title: Protein Sequence Analysis
item.description:
Path: Notes/Bioinformatics/Introdução à Bioinformárica/Protein Sequence Analysis.md
Created: 2024-09-19 13:08
Updated: 2024-09-19 13:08
Source: Protein Sequence Analysis
Subject: Protein Sequence Analysis
Tags:
Ontology parent::Program [Algorithms for Bioinformatics



The flow of the information
From genome to proteome
Always downstream (at that time)
Back in 1956
.
Seminal references
Crick, F.H.C. (1958) On Protein Synthesis. Symp. Soc. Exp. Biol. XII, 139-163​
Crick, F.H.C. (1970) Central dogma of molecular biology. Nature 227(5258):561-563
.



The protein building blocks
Properties matter
Individual contribution
Scope
Neighbourhood
Native protein
Folding may bring toghether distant parts of the polymer
Synergistic effects to the whole folded protein
May be grouped according properties
5 to 7 groups
For most purposes each group may be considered as a general amino acid.
Division in 7 groups
The native residue properties may undergo changes after the synthesis
Post-translational modifications



Structural organization levels
Primary structure
Linear sequence of residues
Secondary structure
Definition "Protein secondary structure is the local spatial conformation of the polypeptide backbone excluding the side chains.[1] The two most common secondary structural elements are alpha helices and beta sheets, though beta turns and omega loops occur as well. Secondary structure elements typically spontaneously form as an intermediate before the protein folds into its three dimensional tertiary structure
Secondary structure is formally defined by the pattern of hydrogen bonds between the amino hydrogen and carboxyl oxygen atoms in the peptide backbone. Secondary structure may alternatively be defined based on the regular pattern of backbone dihedral angles in a particular region of the Ramachandran plot regardless of whether it has the correct hydrogen bonds.
The concept of secondary structure was first introduced by Kaj Ulrik Linderstrøm-Lang at Stanford in 1952.[2][3] Other types of biopolymers such as nucleic acids also possess characteristic secondary structures." Source: Wikipedia
.
Supersecondary structure
Definition "A supersecondary structure is a compact three-dimensional protein structure of several adjacent elements of a secondary structure that is smaller than a protein domain or a subunit. Supersecondary structures can act as nucleations in the process of protein folding."
.

Table with functions of the domains for each anthrax toxin protein. Source: Wikipedia
.
Tertiary structure
The folding of the aminoacid chain.
Definition "Protein tertiary structure is the three-dimensional shape of a protein. The tertiary structure will have a single polypeptide chain "backbone" with one or more protein secondary structures"
.

.
Source: Wikipedia
.
Quaternary structure

.
Definition "is the fourth (and highest) classification level of protein structure. Protein quaternary structure refers to the structure of proteins which are themselves composed of two or more smaller protein chains (also referred to as subunits). Protein quaternary structure describes the number and arrangement of multiple folded protein subunits in a multi-subunit complex. It includes organizations from simple dimers to large homooligomers and complexes with defined or variable numbers of subunits. In contrast to the first three levels of protein structure, not all proteins will have a quaternary structure since some proteins function as single units. Protein quaternary structure can also refer to biomolecular complexes of proteins with nucleic acids and other cofactors."
Source: Wikipedia
.
Quinary structure
The environment meets the protein.
Definition "refers to the features of protein surfaces that are shaped by evolutionary adaptation to the physiological context of living cells. Quinary structure is thus the fifth level of protein complexity, additional to protein primary, secondary, tertiary and quaternary structures. As opposed to the first four levels of protein structure, which are relevant to isolated proteins in dilute conditions, quinary structure emerges from the crowdedness of the cellular context, in which transient encounters among macromolecules are constantly occurring."
Source: Wikipedia
.



The protein as a
Amino acid chain
Linear succession of amino acid residues
A dumb summation of individual contributions
Absent dimensions
Folding
Geometrical neighbours out of the chain
Synergy contribution
No post-transcriptional modifications
Example Tool
ExPASy::ProtParam
Several algorithms implemented
Output varies in quality and reliability



Definition "to break a large polypeptide down into smaller pieces. Proteases are enzymes that typically break peptide bonds by binding to specific amino acid sequences in a protein and catalyzing their hydrolysis.
Chemical reagents, such as cyanogen bromide, which cleaves peptide bonds on the C-terminal side of a methionine residue can also be used to cut larger proteins into smaller peptides. Common proteins performing this activity are found in the digestive system and are shown below.
Subtilisin - C-terminal side of large uncharged side chains
Chymotrypsin - C terminal side of aromatics (Phe, Tyr, Trp)
Trypsin - C-terminal side of lysine and arginines (not next to proline)
Carboxypeptidase - N-terminal side of C-terminal amino acid
Elastase - Hydrolyzes C-side of small AAs (Gly, Ala)
Cyanogen Bromide (chemical) - Hydrolyzes C-side of Met" Source: Biochemistry Free For All (Ahern, Rajagopal, and Tan)
.
Possible use
Modify the polymer
Assess sensitivity to proteases
Detect residue modification
Issues when compared to endonuclease restriction
More elaborated "recognition pattern"
Related to amino acids substitution groups
Wider range of efficacy
Stereospecificity in the access to the peptide bond
Peptide bond
Source: Wikipedia
A role for probabilistic models
Based on composition of the neighbourhood
Expasy::peptidecutter::chymotrypsin/trypsin model
.
Source: Keil, B. "Specificity of proteolysis". Springer-Verlag Berlin-Heidelberg-NewYork, pp.335. (1992)
.
Example Tool
ExPASy::PeptideCutter
Two different operation modes
A simpler model for a wide gamut of cutting agents
A probabilistic and more sophisticated model, for digestion with either
Chymotrypsin
Trypsin

.


Also known as "Transmembrane Domains" (TMD)

Source: Venko 2021et al.
.
Characteristics
Length
19 to 20 aa
Variable composition
Hydropathy required
No consensus sequence
Relevance
Protein biological role
"Uninformative" sequences
Loss of specificity in sequence similarity searches
Technical approaches to the inference of transmembrane regions
No consensus sequence available
But a limited degree of conservation do exist
Trained Hidden Markov Model (HMM) are able to portray the conservation present
Example Implementations
Phobius (SBC)

A second generation HMM for transmembrane region detection

.
Signal peptide discrimination
Provides a refined detection
Numerical results are presented also as a
Plot os probability levels
Conservative interpretation is also supplied
The operator should disregard those
The raw output MUST be assessed for best results

.
DeepTMHMM (DTU)


A "Deep Learning" approach to the problem
The documentation is somewhat vague on the details
The output make the tool look more powerful than Phobius
But the difference may arise from an expanded training set.
The posterior probability plot
.
Gff output
.


Secondary structure does not show a deterministic relation to the sequence
The fuzzy relation can be addressed through
Available knowledge
Artificial intelligence
General workflow
Search for subsequences with secondary structure known
Neural networks
Filtration
Evaluation
General structure for a ann
Example tool
PSIPRED (UCL)
Two step process
Search for subsequences with secondary structure known
PSI-BLAST
Completion, and polishing of the end result
neural networks
Process overview
Sample output
.


Tertiary structure is key for the protein role
No direct deterministic relation to sequence (Primary Structure)
A chimera of subsequences with known folder
Does not make a credible solution to folding inference
A plain energy minimisation for the backbone folding
Fails due to missing contributions
e.g. cofactors
Combining the two approaches
Homology modelling
Example tool
Swiss-Model / DeepView (ExPASy)

.
Deep learning
Example tool
AlphaFold (Google)

.
Risks from not being able to check properly due to mass production
Danger of propagating errors
Database artifact contamination


Detection of discrete structure elements
Case study
Coiled coil
- > [! Definition]
> "A coiled coil is a structural motif in proteins in which 2–7 alpha-helices are coiled together like the strands of a rope. (Dimers and trimers are the most common types.) They have been found in roughly 5-10% of proteins and have a variety of functions. They are one of the most widespread motifs found in protein-protein interactions. (...)
Coiled coils usually contain a repeated pattern, hxxhcxc, of hydrophobic (h) and charged (c) amino-acid residues, referred to as a heptad repeat.[10] The positions in the heptad repeat are usually labeled abcdefg, where a and d are the hydrophobic positions, often being occupied by isoleucine, leucine, or valine." Source: Wikipedia
.
Example Tool
Coils (EMBnet)


Detection of characteristic heptamers
Sliding Windows
Heptamer multiples scored through
2 alternative metrics
Concurrent run for different window sizes
Instant Robustness assessment

.
Example output
.
Detection by absence of defined structure
Disordered stretches along the protein backbone

Fluid folding
Changing with protein environment
Flexible bonding to other proteins
Conformal structure adaptation
Approaches to disorder detection vary
Results seem too dependent on the specific tool employed
The inference of disorder, and its several roles is still a process in development
Example Tools
PSIPRED::DISOPRED3 (UCL)
Detection based on SVM and ANN
Sample output
.
Yield parallel track results for
Disorder
Disorder with binding potential
CAID Prediction Portal (Univ. of Padua)
Meta search
16 tools are consulted
The result map strives for a synthesis of the results, but heterogeneity is the rule
Sample output
.
item.dc:creator: jmgcfdea

item.title: Protocolo_1_Registo_Armazenamento_Sequências
item.description:
Path: Notes/Bioinformatics/Introdução à Bioinformárica/Protocolo_1_Registo_Armazenamento_Sequências.md
Created: 2024-09-05 18:02
Updated: 2024-09-05 18:02
Source: Protocolo_1_Registo_Armazenamento_Sequências
Subject: Protocolo_1_Registo_Armazenamento_Sequências
Tags:
Ontology parent::Program



NotePad++

Description "Based on the powerful editing component Scintilla, Notepad++ is written in C++ and uses pure Win32 API and STL which ensures a higher execution speed and smaller program size."
Main Site
PortableApps
"Runs anywhere (cloud, removable, local) without 'installing'. Use the PortableApps.com Platform for easy installs and automatic updates."
SQLiteMan

SourceForge
PortableApps
SQLiteBrowser (advanced manager)

Description "DB Browser for SQLite (DB4S) is a high quality, visual, open source tool designed for people who want to create, search, and edit SQLite database files. DB4S gives a familiar spreadsheet-like interface on the database in addition to providing a full SQL query facility. It works with Windows, macOS, and most versions of Linux and Unix."
Main Site
Note As usual, Windows systems are advised to go for the portable version.


Protocol
item.dc:creator: jmgcfdea

item.title: Relational Databases
item.description:
Path: Notes/Bioinformatics/Introdução à Bioinformárica/Relational Databases.md
Created: 2024-09-05 14:52
Updated: 2024-09-05 14:52
Source: Relational Databases
Subject: Relational Databases
Tags:
Ontology parent::Program

Concept definition Source: [Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Database)
Concept definition Source: [Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Relational_database)


The Entry
Set of Fields / Attributes
Where Data resides
Before:
After:
The Table
Operational unit of a Relational Database
Before:
Now:
The Database
Set of Tables & required software
Before:
Now:
The Database Management System - DBMS
Software for management & data I/O
Before:
After:


Primary Key (PK)
The exclusive identifier for a single Entry in a specific Table
Unique as identifier
Foreign Key (FK)
The otherwise PK may be used as needed in other Tables as reference to
The referenced Entry
In the source Table
(Table) Join
Relation between two Tables on a PK:FK pair

Fields / Attributes must be atomic
No array storage allowed.
Data redundancy is to be avoided at all costs
To streamline the data structure
To avoid typing errors
Standard Language across different RDBMS
SQL
Selection

Projection

Join (Tables)



Databases can be accessed seamlessly through the World Wide Web

Clusters of different DBMS can be made available through middleware plus client software as single system
item.dc:creator: jmgcfdea

item.title: Sequence Alignment
item.description:
Path: Notes/Bioinformatics/Introdução à Bioinformárica/Sequence Alignment.md
Created: 2024-09-22 17:45
Updated: 2024-09-22 17:45
Source: Sequence Alignment
Subject: Sequence Alignment
Tags:
Ontology parent::Program Sequence Evolution Sequence alignment Global alignment Local alignment



Mapping the putative homologous sites between sequences
Purely mathematical process
Optimize the sequence disposition based on some comparison metric
Alignment challenges
.
Indel
Insertion vs. deletion
.
Used to solve gaps in the comparable sequence
Usually treated as a single event
Use MUST BE PARSIMONIOUS
Free use enables any pair of sequences to yield about 20% similarity, even if totally unrelated
A myriad of possible solutions
.


Sequence alignment can be conducted according two different paradigms
Global Alignment
Definition "identify similarity across the entire length of the sequences."
Source: EDAM
.
End to end
.
The default choice for pair-wise alignment
The SOLE SOLUTION for
Similarity assessment
SNP mapping
Local Alignment
Definition "identify regions of local similarity" Source: EDAM
.
Only most similar subsequences
.
To be chosen whenever
Similarity is known to be localized
Whenever the intrinsic nature of the result is "local"


Non-coincidence? By chance?
The longer the alignment
The higher the confidence in the involvement of homology

.
"the thin red line" Distant homologous matches may require phylogenetic analysis as evidence.
.
item.dc:creator: jmgcfdea

item.title: Sequence Evolution
item.description:
Path: Notes/Bioinformatics/Introdução à Bioinformárica/Sequence Evolution.md
Created: 2024-09-22 13:01
Updated: 2024-09-22 13:01
Source: Sequence Evolution
Subject: Sequence Evolution
Tags:
Ontology parent::Program


Homology, a definition "similarity due to shared ancestry between a pair of structures or genes in different taxa.
(...)
Two segments of DNA can have shared ancestry because of either a speciation event (orthologs) or a duplication event (paralogs). Homology among proteins or DNA is inferred from their sequence similarity. Significant similarity is strong evidence that two sequences are related by divergent evolution from a common ancestor. Alignments of multiple sequences are used to discover the homologous regions." Source: Wikipedia
.
HOMOLOGY is a PROPERTY shared by several sequences, either exists or is absent.
HOMOLOGY is NOT TO BE MEASURED, rather to be assessed.
Homoplasy, definitions "the term used to describe a feature that has been gained or lost independently in separate lineages over the course of evolution. (...)
Homoplasy can arise from both similar selection pressures acting on adapting species, and the effects of genetic drift." Source: Wikipedia
.
"Homoplasy is the development of organs or other bodily structures within different species, which resemble each other and have the same functions, but did not have a common ancestral origin. These organs arise via convergent evolution and are thus analogous, not homologous to each other. For example, the wings of insects, birds and bats, which are all used for flying, are homoplastic (meaning: similar in form and structure, but not in origin)."
.
Source: ScienceDirect
.

Time elapsing makes the difference

.
Orthology
Definition "Homologous sequences are orthologous if they are inferred to be descended from the same ancestral sequence separated by a speciation event: when a species diverges into two separate species, the copies of a single gene in the two resulting species are said to be orthologous. Orthologs, or orthologous genes, are genes in different species that originated by vertical descent from a single gene of the last common ancestor. The term "ortholog" was coined in 1970 by the molecular evolutionist Walter Fitch. (...)
Orthology is strictly defined in terms of ancestry. Given that the exact ancestry of genes in different organisms is difficult to ascertain due to gene duplication and genome rearrangement events, the strongest evidence that two similar genes are orthologous is usually found by carrying out phylogenetic analysis of the gene lineage. Orthologs often, but not always, have the same function.
Orthologous sequences provide useful information in taxonomic classification and phylogenetic studies of organisms. The pattern of genetic divergence can be used to trace the relatedness of organisms. Two organisms that are very closely related are likely to display very similar DNA sequences between two orthologs." Source: Wikipedia
.
Paralogy
Definition "The relationship of any two homologous characters that arose by a genetic duplication." Source: MGI
.
Xenology
Definition "In phylogenetics, the study of genes which have been separated by a horizontal gene transfer event." Source: Oxford Reference
. Horizontal gene transfer, a definition "Horizontal gene transfer (HGT) or lateral gene transfer (LGT) is the movement of genetic material between organisms other than by the ("vertical") transmission of DNA from parent to offspring (reproduction)."
.
Source: Wikipedia
.
Time is absent
Divergence times diverge (divergent phylogenetic trees)
Genome
Sequence resulting from horizontal transfer


Criteria to filter out Homoplasy
Similarity
What is Similarity?
Sequence-wise
Shared sequence sites also present in the closest common ancestor
- Determined by alignment
- Expressed as
- Percentage of identity
If similar enough
Homologous
Conjunction (of similar characters / sequences)
Character
If occurring more than once in the same organism
Analogous
Sequence
If occurring more than once in the same genome
Paralogous
Congruency (for similar characters / sequences)
Consistent evolutions (phylogenies) for both
Considered sequence
Organism / genome where the sequence occurs
Absent
Either
Analogous
Xenologous


Patterson C. Homology in classical and molecular biology. Mol Biol Evol. 1988 Nov;5(6):603-25. [doi: 10.1093/oxfordjournals.molbev.a040523](1patt.pdf (silverchair.com)). PMID: 3065587.
de Pinna, M.C.C. (1991), CONCEPTS AND TESTS OF HOMOLOGY IN THE CLADISTIC PARADIGM. Cladistics, 7: 367-394. https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1096-0031.1991.tb00045.x
item.dc:creator: jmgcfdea

item.title: Sequence Pairwise-Alignment Fast Algorithms, Heuristics to the Rescue
item.description:
Path: Notes/Bioinformatics/Sequence Pairwise-Alignment Fast Algorithms, Heuristics to the Rescue.md
Created: 2024-09-29 17:25
Updated: 2024-09-29 17:25
Source: Sequence Pairwise-Alignment Fast Algorithms, Heuristics to the Rescue
Subject: Sequence Pairwise-Alignment Fast Algorithms, Heuristics to the Rescue
Tags:
Ontology parent::Program Pairwise-Alignment, the Dynamic Programming Role



Slow Algorithms
Algorithms based on Dynamic Programming
Typically explore the whole solutions space
Return the best solution available
But they are inherently slow
Some variants present strategies to slim the solutions space to be explored
The gains are small
The loss of result quality is slight
Fast Algorithms
Algorithms that depart from strict Dynamic Programming
Inclusion of heuristics
Definition "approach to problem solving that employs a pragmatic method that is not fully optimized, perfected, or rationalized, but is nevertheless "good enough" as an approximation or attribute substitution." Source: Wikipedia
.
Drastic reduction of the solutions space to be explored
Relevant compromises on the result quality
Good results versus Optimum results
Alignment speeds compatible with database searching



Definition "The FASTA program follows a largely heuristic method which contributes to the high speed of its execution. It initially observes the pattern of word hits, word-to-word matches of a given length, and marks potential matches before performing a more time-consuming optimized search using a Smith–Waterman type of algorithm." Source: Wikipedia
.
Vocabulary-based alignment
Steps
Listing all the available words in the query sequence
Hashing
k-mer
Peptides
k = 2
Nucleic Acids
k = 4..6
Word-based sequence database retrieval
Sequence pool to be aligned
Entries for sequences vocabulary with the query
For each matchable sequence
Word occurrence map
Word-pair relevance
Assessed through PAM250
Definition of a diagonal band
Incorporating the best matches
Extension of word pairs
Limited to the 10 highest-scoring
Extension through formal alignement
Needle-Wunsch algorithm
Statistical assessment
Empirical evaluation for the Level of Significance
Summary
.


Strict vocabulary-based alignment
Steps
Listing all the available words in the query sequence
Hashing
k-mer
Peptides
k = 3
Nucleic Acids
k = 12
Vocabulary enrichment
Observed vocabulary
Hashing
.
Source: Researchgate
.
Neighborhood words
Through BLOSUM62
Words absent from the observed set
Finding words that also matter Source: Researchgate
.
Similar enough to each one of the observed k-mer
From high scoring words to high scoring pairs Source: NCBI
.
Mapping High Scoring Pairs (HSP) & Extending into Maximum Scoring Pairs (MSP)
Assigning high scoring words to high scoring pairs Source: Researchgate
.
Extending hsp into maximum scoring pairs “Pasted image 20240930151726.png” could not be found.
.
Finishing the alignment without formal alignment
Extending the pairing until reasonable Source: Researchgate
.
Final alignment & statistics generation
Blast output Source: Researchgate
.
Graphical Abstract

.


BLAST is implemented in different variants that receive a specific kind of input, and match it against a defined type of databases
Main variants Note: TBLASTX matches the translations of the query to the translations of the targets.
.
item.guid: Notes/Bioinformatics/Introdução à Bioinformática/Sequence Pairwise-Alignment Fast Algorithms, Heuristics to the Rescue.md
item.dc:creator: jmgcfdea

item.title: The Substitution Matrix
item.description:
Path: Notes/Bioinformatics/Introdução à Bioinformárica/The Substitution Matrix.md
Created: 2024-09-27 04:59
Updated: 2024-09-27 04:59
Source: The Substitution Matrix
Subject: The Substitution Matrix
Tags:
Ontology parent::Program Sequence Alignment Sequence Evolution



How to score different differences?

.
A scoring metric that reflects evolution probability through substitutions between extant residues
The Substitution Matrix
The blosum62
.


General content
- The score assigned to each substitution meant by the crossing between column and row amino acids.
- > [! Fragment]
>
> .
- Substitution
- > [! Definition]
> "" [[]]
-From the simplest...
- Identity Matrix
- Only scores the no substitution condition
- > [! Identity Matrix]
>
> .
- Destined to situations where similar is restricted to being equal
- Nucleic acid residues matching
- It may include IUPAC ambiguity symbols
- Values may differ from [0,1]
- e.g. BLASTN
...through the discontinued...
Genetic Code Matrix
Substitution score following the minimum number of nucleotide changes required to turn the codon from coding one amino acid to the other
higher scores for conserved CDSs
Did not prove to be better than more sound models
Discontinued

.
...to the PAM revolution and...
Observable evolution, Margaret Dayhoff et al. 1978
Margaret dayhoff
.
Dataset (from the Atlas of Protein Sequence and Structure)
Groups of proteins
71
Shared identity
85% (very close)
Substitutions found
1572
Procedure
Phylogenetic analysis
Ancestor sequence reconstruction
Log Odd Ratio calculations for each studied substitution
Pam log odd ratio
.
Product
MDM/PAM 1
Describes the situation for
A tiny amount of evolution
Sequences 99% similar
About
10 million years of evolution time
Covers an evolution span too small to be really useful.
Higher grade PAM matrices
Much more useful
Derived from PAM1
e.g. PAM120

.
PAM250
Suitable to score substitutions sharing between 40 to 60% identity
May be used as a
Default matrix for prior use
Gonnet Matrices
The same approach
Wider dataset
... then back to an empirical solution
BLOSUM Matrices
Henikoff & Henikoff 1992
Substrate to build on
Set of sequences sharing a given precise identity (~62%) maximum threshold
The set is aligned
Blocks are identified along the alignment
Consecutive sites (columns) that does not include gaps
Most conserved regions of the alignment

All substitutions present in all blocks are assessed
Calculation of Log Odd Ratio over the whole substitutions dataset
Log odd ratio
.
Practical
The numerical suffix of each matrix indicates
The intended identity for the sequences that can be processed with this BLOSUM matrix.
Powerful
The results obtained through the employment of these matrices seem better that even with MDM / PAM
item.dc:creator: jmgcfdea

item.title: Translated Regions Detection
item.description:
Path: Notes/Bioinformatics/Introdução à Bioinformárica/Translated Regions Detection.md
Created: 2024-09-16 15:59
Updated: 2024-09-16 15:59
Source: Translated Regions Detection
Subject: Translated Regions Detection
Tags:
Ontology parent::Algorithms for Bioinformatics Program Coding region prediction Ab-initio gene prediction Genetic code Hidden Markov model Workflows GFF3



Where to begin?
.


Gene gene
Definition “A unit of DNA which performs one function. Usually, this is equated with the production of one RNA or one protein. A gene contains coding regions, introns, untranslated regions and control regions.”
Source: EBI::2can
.
Includes
Coding regions
Introns
Non-translated regions
Regulatory regions
Coding Sequence (CDS)
Definition “The portion of a gene or an mRNA which actually codes for a protein. Introns are not coding sequences; nor are the 5' or 3' untranslated regions (or the flanking regions, for that matter - they are not even transcribed into mRNA).”
Source: EBI::2can
.
Limitations
Sequence based
Introns excluded
Only protein-coding genes

.
Open Reading Frame (ORF)
Definition “A sequence of successive nucleotide triplets that are read as CODONS specifying AMINO ACIDS and begin with an INITIATOR CODON and end with a stop codon ( CODON, TERMINATOR).”
Source: NCBI(http://www.nlm.nih.gov/cgi/mesh/2009/ ; UniqueID: D016366)
.
Limitations
Codon-dependent reading
Similar to CDS, but usually used as reading interpretation.

Expressed Sequence Tag (EST)
Definition “A small sequence from an expressed gene that can be amplified by PCR. ESTs act as physical markers for cloning and full length sequencing of the cDNAs of expressed genes. Typically identified by purifying mRNAs, converting to cDNAs, and then sequencing a portion of the cDNAs. .”
Source: EBI::2can
.
Making of
.
Visualized
.


Phylogenetic Domains
Different challenges
Gene density
Bacteria
~1 gene / kbp
CDS represent circa 70%
M. tuberculosis
.
Eukarya
~0.01 gene / kbp
CDS represent circa 5%
[! M. musculus]
Detail

.
Chromosome geometry
Bacteria
Circular
Eukarya
Linear
ORF arrangement
Eukarya
May present Introns
The ORF may be present across the 3 available Reading Frames.
Both chains may present concurrent genes
Transcription may vary for different conditions for the same gene
Bacteria
Uninterrupted
No concurrent coding in both chains
Bacterial operon
Heterologous sequences may be present
Temperate phages
Transposons
Supplemental data availability
Homologous sets of
Protein sequences
cDNA or EST libraries



Orfing
Mapping Start and Stop Codons.
Using a suitable Genetic Code
For each available Reading Frame
3 per Chain


Stretches of sequence without a Stop codon, and beginning with a Start codon
Putative ORF
The longer the putative ORF
The more plausible its existence will be
Small ORFs
Even real
Will not be considered.
Typical success rate
< 85% of the real ORFs present
Too low to be really useful.
Limitations
No introns may be present
Example Implementation
NCBI::ORF Finder

.
Matching to Previously Trained Hidden Markov Model (HMM)
Probabilistic model that relates tangible states (observable) to hidden ones conveying a feature (e.g. being a gene)
A gentle introduction
Getting serious
In fact
Training of the Models is crucial for the result quality


Nobody relies on ab initio tools anymore

.
Workflows feeding a pipeline may enhance the quality of the prediction by chaining
Masker for putative heterologous sequences (RepeatMasker)
Parallel ab initio tools
e.g. Augustus, SNAP, etc
Evidence based gene prediction (proteins, EST, etc)
Consolidation of all the prediction through consensus
Refinement of the boundaries of the predictions (e.g. Exonerate)
Unified annotation for the sequence

.
Complexity is not a substitute for training quality



Formats
Requirement for being open-ended
Preserving the ability to grow in a organic fashion
The sequence may be absent
Case Study
GFF3
Tabular nature

Hierarchical Features
Molecule
Gene
CDS
Exon
Transcript
UTR
etc
Example
.
Intended for being visualized graphically
Generic example
.
Example Tools
SANGER::Artemis
Mainly a java based editor
GMOD::Apollo
Intended for server-side
item.dc:creator: jmgcfdea
